coredata.dc:title: Construction of a ratiometric fluorescent probe for visual detection of urea in human urine based on carbon dots prepared from Toona sinensis leaves and 5-carboxyfluorescein
coredata.dc:creator: Bei, Yuyang
Arkin, Kamile
Zheng, Yuxin
Ma, Xuesong
Zhao, Jie
Jin, Huimin
Shang, Qingkun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, pH-sensitive blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (17.24%) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using Toona sinensis leaves and ethylenediamine (EDA) as raw materials. The CDs can detect urea with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6.700 mmol L−1. For more sensitive detection of urea, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe (CDs@5-FAM) using CDs and 5-carboxyfluorescein (5-FAM). The CDs@5-FAM probe can rapidly and sensitively detect urea according to the changes of I514/I405, with LOD as low as 0.014 mmol L−1. Furthermore, with the help of a smartphone and RGB analysis software, urea's visual intelligent detection was realized using a CDs@5-FAM probe. The method proposed in this paper is consistent with the standard method, which indicates that the pH-sensitive ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs@5-FAM is accurate and reliable for practical application. It provides a new way for rapid and visual detection of urea.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Intelligent and visual
Urea
Carbon dots
Toona sinensis leaves

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the CDs@5-FAM fluorescent probe
Detection of urea by CDs or CDs@5-FAM probe
Detection of urea in human urine
The structure and properties of CDs
Construction of the CDs@5-FAM ratiometric fluorescent probe
Detection of urea in human urine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22172021 , 21872022 , 21573039 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) (The inset is the HRTEM image), particle size distribution (B), Raman spectrum (C), FTIR spectrum (D), XPS spectrum (E) of CDs. (F–I) are the XPS high-resolution spectra of C, O, N, and S, respectively.

UV–vis absorption, optimal excitation and emission spectra of CDs (A). (The inset is photograph under visible light or under 365 nm UV lamp.) Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths (B). Fluorescence decay curve of CDs (C). Fluorescence spectra as pH increased from 3 to 13 (D) (The inset shows the relationship between I/I0 and pH. Where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in stock solution and different pH buffer solution).

Effect of fluorescence intensity of CDs by urea with different concentration(A) and the linear relationship between the normalized fluorescence intensity (I/I0) with urea concentration (0.005–0.100 mol L−1) (B). The urease concentration was 200 μg mL−1 and the total volume was 1 mL.
The fluorescence spectra of 5-FAM at different pH (A) and excitation wavelength (B), the fluorescence spectra (C) and UV–vis absorption spectra (D) of CDs@5-FAM.
Fluorescence spectra (λex = 320 nm) of CDs@5-FAM with the addition of different concentrations of urea (0–1 mmol L−1) and 2.0 μg mL−1 urease at pH 3.0 (A), 3.5 (B) and 4.0 (C).

The effect of increasing urea concentration on fluorescence spectra of four ratiometric fluorescence probe under four different I405 conditions (The volume of CDs are 150 μL, 250 μL, 350 μL, and 450 μL, respectively). (Insets are their corresponding photographs taken under a 320 nm UV lamp).

Selectivity of towards 12 potential interfering substances (A). Histogram of CDs@5-FAM's selectivity and anti-interference ability. The black column indicates the selectivity of the probe to different substances and the red column indicates the anti-interference ability of the probe to different substances (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic diagram of urea detection using the developed smartphone integrated sensing platform (A). Fluorescence photos of the CDs@5-FAM based fluorescence sensing system upon the addition of diverse concentrations of urea under 320 nm UV lamp (B). Linear relationship plots of G/B values versus urea concentrations (C). (Urea concentrations: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 mmol L−1).
Detection of urea in human urine (n = 3).

row.entry: Detected by SP b method (mmol L−1)
Found by FL a method (mmol L−1)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: a means via I514/I405; b means via spectrophotometric.

head.ce:title: Construction of a ratiometric fluorescent probe for visual detection of urea in human urine based on carbon dots prepared from Toona sinensis leaves and 5-carboxyfluorescein

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, 130024, Changchun, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, 130024, Changchun, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, pH-sensitive blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (17.24%) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using Toona sinensis leaves and ethylenediamine (EDA) as raw materials. The CDs can detect urea with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6.700 mmol L−1. For more sensitive detection of urea, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe (CDs@5-FAM) using CDs and 5-carboxyfluorescein (5-FAM). The CDs@5-FAM probe can rapidly and sensitively detect urea according to the changes of I514/I405, with LOD as low as 0.014 mmol L−1. Furthermore, with the help of a smartphone and RGB analysis software, urea's visual intelligent detection was realized using a CDs@5-FAM probe. The method proposed in this paper is consistent with the standard method, which indicates that the pH-sensitive ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs@5-FAM is accurate and reliable for practical application. It provides a new way for rapid and visual detection of urea.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Urea is the main product of protein metabolism and is mainly secreted by the human kidney and liver [1]. Urea levels in urine and blood are often used as one of the essential indicators to check liver and kidney function and human metabolic function [2]. Urea exists in various body fluids such as blood, urine, and saliva [3], and the concentration of urea in human urine (275–409 mmol L−1) is much higher than that in human blood (2.50–7.14 mmol L−1) [4]. Therefore, collecting urine in a non-invasive manner and assessing a person's physical condition by urea content is more direct and safer than collecting blood samples. Capillary electrophoresis [5,6], electrochemical methods [7–9], and chromatography [10] can be used for the quantitative analysis of urea, which has the characteristics of high sensitivity and low detection limit, but usually require expensive equipment and complex preprocessing. Therefore, developing a rapid and simple method to determine urea content for early screening of certain diseases is necessary.

The ratiometric fluorescent probe method is an analytical method to determine the target's content by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratio at two different emission wavelengths as a signal parameter. Since the measured fluorescence ratio signal is not affected by light source intensity and instrument sensitivity. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescent probes have high sensitivity, selectivity, and linear range. In recent years, some ratiometric fluorescent probes have been applied to detect various ions [11], biomass [12], and tumor markers [13], which are simple, fast, and sensitive.

Since their first successful synthesis in 2004, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in sensing [14,15], photocatalysis [16], optoelectronic devices [17,18], bioimaging [19,20], and other fields due to their excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, and accessible surface functionalization. There are many raw materials that can be used to prepare CDs. Among them, biomass materials have the advantages of vast sources and low cost. Many reports have been synthesized CDs based on biomass materials rich in C, N, O, and other elements [21–23]. However, biomass CDs often have the disadvantages of the single color and low fluorescence quantum yield, and other chemical reagents are usually required to obtain sensitive ratiometric fluorescent probes. For example, Cui et al. [24] synthesized biomass carbon quantum dots (BQDs) with single excitation and double emission characteristics using chlorophyll extracted from cabbage and polyoxyethylene diamine as raw materials. In the presence of Cu2+, the obtained BQDs can be used for near-infrared ratiometric fluorescence detection of coenzyme A (CoA) and realize ratiometric fluorescence imaging of CoA in living cells. Sun et al. [25] prepared blue CDs (B-CQDs) from the skin of thyme. B-CQDs were combined with Eu3+ to produce a proportional fluorescence response to tetracycline (TC). On this basis, a proportional fluorescent molecularly imprinted probe B-CQDs@Eu/MIPs was prepared to recognize TC specifically.

In this work, we synthesized blue CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method using Toona sinensis leaves as carbon source and EDA as nitrogen source. Toona sinensis (A. Juss.) Roem is a tree of Toona family and Toona genus. It is native to central and southern China and has been cultivated in many places, including my hometown of Shandong. The new tender shoots are edible, but the grown toona leaves are not. Toona sinensis is rich in calcium, phosphorus, potassium, sodium, vitamin E, vitamin C, carotene, sex hormones and volatile aromatic organic compounds, with a variety of medicinal effects. CDs with higher fluorescence quantum yield were obtained by using Toona leaves with such rich composition as carbon source. Based on the sensitive response of the as-prepared CDs to pH of the solution, combined with 5-FAM, the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs@5-FAM was successfully constructed. The probe has an excellent linear relationship between the change of I514/I405 and the urea content, which can be used to detect urea content in human urine rapidly. The detection limit (LOD) was as low as 0.014 mmol L−1. In addition, a new method for visual detection of urea using a smartphone applet was developed by taking advantage of the ratiometric fluorescent probe changing from blue to green under ultraviolet light with increasing urea concentration. The work of this paper is of great significance for the further development and utilization of biomass CDs in the application of medical detection.

Toona sinensis leaves were taken from a village in Shandong Province. Ethylenediamine (EDA), quinine sulfate, urease, glucose (Glu), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), l-phenylalanine (L-Phen) and serine (Ser) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (China). 5-carboxyfluorescein (5-FAM), urea, uric acid and creatinine were purchased from Macklin Company (Shanghai, China). Ammonium chloride, calcium chloride, sodium chloride, potassium nitrate, sodium bicarbonate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium phosphate were bought from Beijing Chemical Plant.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of CDs were tested by transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 PLUS, Japan. Test conditions: accelerated voltage 200 KV, point resolution 0.19 nm, line resolution 0.1 nm). Raman spectra was recorded by Raman spectroscopy (Labram Hr Evolution, Japan. Test conditions: laser power 100 mW, acquisition time 30 s, grating 1800 (500 nm)). The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700，USA) was used to test the infrared absorption spectra of samples (Spectral range 4000–400 cm−1, resolution 0.125 cm−1). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA, Al target, electron gun beam spot 500 nm, spatial resolution 3 μm). Ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Cary 7000, USA) was used to analyze the characteristic absorption of samples (Spectral range 200–600 nm, sweeping speed is slow, the excitation light source is a xenon lamp). The fluorescence spectra were measured using fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320, China. Test conditions: integration time 1 s, scanning speed 1200 nm/min, slit width 10 nm, cuvette path length 1 cm). Fluorescence lifetime of sample was determined using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK. The excitation and emission wavelengths were monitored at 367 nm and 439 nm, respectively).

CDs were obtained through hydrothermal treatment of toona sinensis leaf powder and EDA. Toona sinensis leaves were freeze-dried and ground into powder before use. 110 μL EDA (m = 0.1 g) and 0.7 g toona sinensis leaf powder were dissolved in 10 mL H2O. The mixture solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. It was heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, a brown solution was obtained. The brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, filtered through a 0.22 μm filter, and stored in a 4 °C refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the CDs@5-FAM fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: First, 5-FAM was dispersed in 20 mL of phosphate buffer solution (10 mmol L−1, pH = 3.5), and the concentration of 5-FAM was fixed at 1.25 μg mL−1. Then, different volumes of CDs were added to the dispersion of 5-FAM, and the mixed solution was stirred in the dark for 12 h to obtain CDs@5-FAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urea by CDs or CDs@5-FAM probe
ce:section.ce:para: By CDs: 100 μL urease (200 μg mL−1) was mixed with 100 μL urea solution of different concentrations and incubated in an oven at 25 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, 800 μL of CDs (23 μg mL−1) was added, and the urea concentration was examined by measuring the change of I440.

By CDs@5-FAM: 100 μL urease (200 μg mL−1) was mixed with 100 μL urea solution of different concentrations and incubated in an oven at 25 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, 800 μL of CDs@5-FAM (7.29 μg mL−1) was added, and the urea concentration was examined by measuring the change in fluorescence intensity ratio I514/I405.

Some common inorganic salts in the human body such as calcium chloride, sodium phosphate, sodium chloride and potassium nitrate and several substances that may exist in human urine such as Ala, Gly, Leu, L-Phen, Ser, creatinine, uric acid, glucose have been selected as interfering substances. Except for uric acid, which was 0.1 mmol L−1, the concentrations of interfering substances were all 1.0 mmol L−1. These interfering substances were added to the ratiometric fluorescent probe solution instead of urea solution or simultaneously with urea, and the selectivity of the probe to urea was investigated. All experiments were repeated 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urea in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: Urine samples from two healthy young women were taken and diluted 100-fold for urea determination. After incubating 100 μL of urine sample with 100 μL of urease for 10 min, it was mixed with 800 μL of CDs@5-FAM, and the changes in fluorescence intensity I514/I405 were measured to calculate the urea content. Standard solutions of urea with different concentrations were added to the urine samples, and in the same way, the recovery rate of standard addition was calculated according to the determination results.
For the same sample, use a smartphone to read the color information and convert it into digital information to calculate the urea content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). As seen in Fig. 1 (A), the CDs are spheroidal and well separated from each other. HRTEM (insert Fig. 1(A)) shows that the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating that the CDs contain a graphitized structure [26]. The CDs have a size distribution from 1 to 4 nm with an average diameter of 2.5 nm (Fig. 1(B)). The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1(C)) shows two peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1583 cm−1, attributed to the D band (from disorder) and G band (from the sp2 carbon network in the graphite structure), respectively. The intensity ratio ID/IG is 0.78, indicating the presence of graphene-like structure in the synthesized CDs [27].

The functional groups and chemical composition of the CDs surface were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1(D)), the characteristic peak at 3340-3140 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H, the peak at 2940 cm−1 to the stretching vibration of C–H, and the characteristic absorption peak at 1670 cm−1 to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO or amide. Additionally, the peak at 1410 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of N–H, and 1580, 1290, 1110, 1040 cm−1 correspond to the absorption peaks of CC, C–N, C–O, and C–S, respectively.

The XPS analysis revealed that the prepared CDs was primarily composed of four elements: C, O, N, and S (Fig. 1(E)), in the proportions of C: 71.24%, O: 22.28%, N: 5.31%, and S:1.17%. There are three distinct peaks of C–C/CC (284.6 eV), C–N/C–O/C–S (285.2 eV) and CO (288.0 eV) in the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1(F)), among which C–C/CC is dominant. This indicates the presence of substantial graphitic-like structures [28]. The O1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1(G)) can be fitted into two peaks, which are respectively attributed to CO (531.5 eV) and C–OH (533.2 eV), where CO is the main form of oxygen in CDs. In the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 1(H)), the N1s band can be fitted with three peaks at 398.9 eV, 399.8 eV and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyridine N, N–H and pyrrole N. It is clear that N–H is the main existence form of N element. The characteristic peaks at 163.8 eV and 168.0 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 1(I)) correspond to C–S and C-SOx. From the above analysis, it can be concluded that the abundant vitamins and amino acids in Toona sinensis leaves lead to the existence of abundant nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [29].

In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (shown in Fig. 2 (A)), there are two distinct absorption peaks at 274 nm and 320 nm. The absorption peak at 274 nm may be derived from the π-π* transition of the conjugated system in the carbon core, and the wide absorption at 320 nm can be regarded as the surface state, corresponding to the n-π* transition of C–O or C–N functional groups on the CDs surface [30]. CDs can be excited at 367 nm and its emission maximum wavelength is 439 nm. Aqueous solutions of CDs are pale yellow under sunlight and bright blue under UV light at 365 nm (insert in Fig. 2(A)). Fig. 2(B) shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength was increased from 307 nm to 467 nm, the emission peak of the CDs shifted from 410 nm to 520 nm. The fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased, and the fluorescence emission was the strongest when the excitation wavelength was 367 nm. This indicates that the CDs are excitation-dependent, which may be caused by CDs of different sizes (quantum effect) and the different surface states came from different functional groups on the surface [31,32]. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs is about 6.15 ns (Fig. 2(C)). In addition, from Fig. S1, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs is calculated to be 17.24% by the five-point method, such a high fluorescence quantum yield is rare in CDs prepared from biomass.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs under different pH conditions (λex = 367 nm) are shown in Fig. 2(D). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs is almost the same when the pH is increased from 3 to 10. As the pH continued to increase from 10 to 13, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly. In addition, the position of the CDs emission peak was red-shifted from 439 nm to 447 nm at pH from 10 to 13. When the pH of the CDs solution is adjusted from 13 return to 10, the fluorescence intensity is almost completely restored. After 5 cycles, the decrease value of CDs fluorescence intensity was basically the same, and the recovery value only decreased by about 9% (as shown in Fig. S2(A)). This shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs is reversible with pH changes in the range of 10–13. To find out the reason for the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, the relationship between zeta potential and pH was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2(B), with the increase of pH, the Zeta potential of CDs became more negative. In the range of pH 3–10, the Zeta potential did not change much. However, in the process of increasing pH 10 to 13, the Zeta potential of CDs became negative obviously. This may be the result of deprotonation of –COOH or –NH3 + on the surface of CDs, together with the protonation of –NH2, resulting in that the fluorescence intensity of CDs can decrease and then recover [33]. The electron-withdrawing ability of the deprotonated carboxyl group is weakened, thus red-shifting the fluorescence emission peak of CDs.

The effects of storage time, ionic solution strength, and illumination time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were further studied. After one month of storage in a 4 °C refrigerator, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was almost unchanged (Fig. S3(A)). When the ionic strength of the solution was gradually increased (indicated by the increase of NaCl concentration from 0.2 to 1.0 mol L−1), the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained constant (Fig. S3(B)). The CDs solution was continuously irradiated under a 367 nm UV lamp for 60 min, and the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly (Fig. S3(C)). The above experimental results show that the prepared CDs have good temporal stability, salt-resistance, and photobleaching resistance.

As we know, urea can be hydrolyzed in the presence of urease according to equation:
U r e a +
H 2
0
→
U r e a s e
H C
O 3
−
+ O
H −
+ 2 N
H 4 +

The hydrolysis of urea will inevitably increase the pH of the solution, which may weaken or quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Can this be used to detect the content of urea in the solution? From Fig. 3 (A), it was found that when the concentration of urea increased from 0.005 to 0.100 mol L−1 in 1 mL of solution (containing 200 μg of urease), the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased by about 20%. There is an excellent linear relationship between its normalized fluorescence intensity (I/I0) and the concentration of urea (Fig. 3(B)):
(1)
y = 0.9352 − 1.5931 x
(
R 2
= 0.9827
)
where y is normalized fluorescence intensity (I/I0), and x is the concentration of urea. Based on this, it can be calculated that the LOD of CDs detection of urea is 6.700 mmol L−1.

Although the fluorescence quenching of CDs can be used to detect the content of urea, the sensitivity is poor. Due to the low recognition of the brightness of single-color fluorescence, it is difficult to accurately observe the change of urea concentration with the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the CDs@5-FAM ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Since CDs has essentially the same fluorescence intensity in the pH range of 3–10, this means that it is suitable as an internal standard moiety for constructing pH-sensing ratiometric fluorescent probes. If we can find molecules with large changes in fluorescence intensity in this pH range, it will be possible to construct suitable ratiometric fluorescent probes. The fluorescence spectra of 5-FAM under different pH conditions were investigated (as shown in Fig. 4 (A)), and it was found that the fluorescence intensity of 5-FAM was weaker under acidic conditions and stronger under alkaline conditions. After normalization, it was found that the fluorescence intensity increased rapidly when the pH value increased from 4 to 8. The maximum value is reached when the pH is greater than 8. There was only a tiny change in the fluorescence intensity as the pH continued to increase. As shown in Fig. 4(B), the emission peak position of 5-FAM remained unchanged when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 420 nm. The maximum excitation wavelength is 320 nm. Based on this, a CDs@5-FAM ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed with CDs as the internal standard part and 5-FAM as the OH− sensitive part, and 320 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength of the ratiometric fluorescent probe.

Fig. 4(C) shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs@5-FAM obtained by the same concentration of CDs and 5-FAM. When the excitation wavelength was 320 nm, CDs@5-FAM showed two emission peaks at 405 nm and 514 nm, corresponding to CDs and 5-FAM, respectively. The fluorescence intensity at 405 nm was marginally lower than that of CDs alone, and the fluorescence intensity at 514 nm was slightly higher than that of 5-FAM alone. From Fig. 4(D), it can be found that 5-FAM has an absorption peak at 400–500 nm. Therefore, there is an energy transfer between CDs and 5-FAM. This is the reason for the weakened fluorescence emission at 405 nm and the enhanced fluorescence emission at 514 nm of CDs@5-FAM.

It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@5-FAM probe remained constant after continuous irradiation with 320 nm light for 60 min or after 30 days in the refrigerator at 4 °C (Fig. S4(A) and (B)). This indicates that the CDs@5-FAM probe has good stability, which lays a solid foundation for further research and practical application.

The initial pH environment to construct the probe is crucial to obtain high sensitivity of color change during urea detection. Controlling the initial pH values of 3.0, 3.5, and 4.0, the effect of increasing urea concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@5-FAM probe was investigated (listed in Fig. 5 ). As the concentration of urea increased from 0 to 1 mmol L−1, the emission peak intensity of CDs@5-FAM probe at 405 nm is always greater than that at 514 nm, that is, the system continuously emits blue fluorescence. On the contrary, when the pH is 4.0, the emission peak intensity of CDs@5-FAM probe at 514 nm is always greater than that at 405 nm; that is, the system continuously emits green fluorescence. Only when the pH was 3.5, with the increase of urea concentration, did the system change from blue fluorescence to green fluorescence. Therefore, pH 3.5 is a more suitable initial pH condition for the probe.

Similarly, the ratio of CDs and 5-FAM in the CDs@5-FAM probe has a significant influence on the sensitivity of the color change during the detection of urea because it directly determines the ratio of the emission peak intensity of the system at 405 nm and 514 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity at 405 nm (I405) can be regulated by changing the amounts of CDs added when the amount of 5-FAM is fixed. Fig. 6 presents the results of the effect of increasing urea concentration on probe fluorescence spectra under four different I405 conditions, denoted as probe 1 to probe 4, respectively. It is clear that with the increase of the urea concentration, the fluorescence colors of the fluorescent probes 1 to 4 change from light green to dark green (Fig. 6(A) for probe 1), from blue to bright green (Fig. 6(B) for probe 2), from blue to light green (Fig. 6(C) for probe 3), and from dark blue to turquoise (Fig. 6(D) for probe 4). In the above four cases, only the fluorescence color of proportional fluorescent probe 2 changed obviously, which was regarded as the best probe system.

Further, we found an excellent linear relationship (shown in Fig. S5 between the change in the intensity ratio (I514/I405) of the ratiometric fluorescent probe and the urea concentration. For urea concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 mmol L−1, the associated equation：
(2)
y = − 0.1102 + 1.5771
x
(
R 2
= 0.9982
)

Here, y is equal to the difference between I514/I405 in any situation and (I514/I405)0 without urea, and x is the urea concentration. According to 3σ/slope, the lowest LOD for urea was calculated to be 0.014 mmol L−1. Compared with pure CDs, the sensitivity of the CDs@5-FAM probe was increased by 478-fold. Compared with the fluorescence method listed in Table S1, the CDs@5-FAM method has the lowest LOD and the response time is greatly shortened.

Selectivity and anti-interference ability are the keys to reflecting the practical application value of probes. The response characteristics of the CDs@5-FAM probe to certain inorganic salts, amino acids, creatinine, glucose, and uric acid that may exist in urine were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 (A), in addition to urea, which could significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity at 514 nm, 12 substances (l-alanine, glycine, leucine, l-phenylalanine, serine, CaCl2, Na3PO4, NaCl, KNO3, creatinine, uric acid, glucose) did not respond. It shows that the probe has good selectivity for the detection of urea. Fig. 7(B) is a bar graph showing the effects of these 12 substances on the ratiometric fluorescent probe I514/I405 when coexisting with urea, respectively. It can be seen that the coexisting substances do not affect I514/I405; that is, they do not interfere with the detection of urea.

Under 320 nm UV light irradiation, the ratiometric fluorescent probe emitted blue to green fluorescence when the urea concentration was increased from 0 to 1 mmol L−1. Although the color change can be observed with the naked eye, it is difficult to give the actual content of urea directly. With the help of a smartphone, the color information is converted into digital information after taking pictures of the reaction system between the probe and urea, and then analyzed using the unique application program of the mobile phone. The operation process is shown in Fig. 8 . We use an iPhone 13 with the ColorColl app for portable urea analysis. The standard urea solutions with different concentrations were hydrolyzed by urease and then added to CDs@5-FAM. Take a series of photos with a smartphone from blue to green under 320 nm UV light. The UV flashlight (320 nm) is fixed at 40 cm away from the cuvette to take the shot. In order to avoid the difference of personal ability to take photos and the impact of different smartphone parameters on data, the smartphone parameters are set as follows: lens focal length is 26 mm, aperture is f1.6, exposure time is 1/17s, pixel is 12 MP and photo size is 3024 × 4032. ColorColl can obtain the G/B values corresponding to these photos, and a linear relationship between the G/B value and the urea concentration is established:
(3)
y = 0.9402 + 0.8867
x
(
R 2
= 0.9992
)
where y is the ratio of G to B and x is the urea concentration. The operation procedure of the actual sample is the same. First, download the ColorColl app from the smartphone app store. Second, take photos with smart phones and upload the photos on the main interface of ColorColl. Third, click the “Color Reading Area” to obtain the GB value of the photos. Finally, substitute G/B value into the standard curve and obtain the urea content. The lowest detection limit was calculated to be 0.019 mmol L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urea in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: Take two volunteer urine samples, dilute them 100 times, and then measure (I514/I405) and (I514/I405)0, respectively, according to the method in section 2.7, and bring them into formula (2) to calculate the urea content. Add standard urea solutions of different concentrations to the samples for determination, and calculate the recovery rate of standard addition. The average of the three test results is listed in Table 1 . The data showed that the recovery rate of standard addition was above 93.00%, and the maximum relative standard deviation was 5.61%. Next, the photo of urine sample under 320 nm ultraviolet light after the interaction with the probe was taken with a smartphone (the photos are shown in Fig. S6), converted into a digital signal, and brought into equation (3) to calculate the urea content. The results are also listed in Table 1. Obviously, the obtained results of urea content and fluorescence ratio are basically consistent. In addition, the urea content in these two samples and the spiked samples was determined by spectrophotometry (standard method). The assay results are very close to the two assays using CDs@5-FAM, with a maximum relative deviation of 4.83%. This indicates that the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs@5-FAM proposed in this paper is accurate and reliable for determining of urea in human urine. It is feasible to realize the visual detection of urea content using a smartphone.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs prepared from biomass is usually low, which limits their further applications. In this paper, CDs with high quantum yield (17.24%) were successfully prepared by co-hydrothermal treatment of Toona sinensis leaves and EDA. A pH-sensitive dual emission ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed by combining the prepared CDs with 5-FAM, which increased the detection sensitivity of urea by 478 times. On this basis, with the help of a smartphone and related applications program, the intelligent visual detection of urea is realized. None of the possible substances in urine interfered with the detection of urea by the CDs@5-FAM probe. The detection results of urea using the CDs@5-FAM probe was consistent with the standard method recommended by the state. In conclusion, the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs@5-FAM constructed in this paper has the advantages of rapidity, sensitivity, and good selectivity for the detection of urea and will have excellent application prospects in future medical detection.

Yuyang Bei: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Kamile Arkin: Methodology, Investigation. Yuxin Zheng: Methodology, Investigation. Xuesong Ma: Software, Visualization. Jie Zhao: Conceptualization, Validation. Huimin Jin: Validation, Visualization. Qingkun Shang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172021, 21872022, 21573039).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.340733.
